message.content:
Team, I'm the Driver for Step 7 -- Clarify as Project. I've read all the required documents, verified the baseline is green (70 unit + 183 integration + 11 Playwright = 264 tests), and studied the existing code patterns. Before coding, I want to discuss the interaction design. Here's my proposal: ## Current State Right now, each inbox item has two inline actions: "Next Action" (with a context select) and "Trash". The clarify form POSTs to `/inbox/{id}/clarify` with `context_id`. ## Proposed Design for "Clarify as Project" I propose adding a **"Project" button** alongside the existing "Next Action" button. When clicked via standard form submission, it POSTs to a new endpoint `/inbox/{id}/clarify-as-project`. The key question: **What fields does "Clarify as Project" need?** Per the spec, clarifying as a project: 1. Creates a Project (Active) using the inbox item's title as the project title 2. Creates a first NextAction (Active) linked to that project, requiring a **title** and **context** 3. Deletes the inbox item So the user needs to provide: - **First action title** (text input) - **Context** for the first action (select) ### Interaction options: **Option A -- Inline expansion (CSS `:has()` progressive disclosure):** Each inbox item gets a radio group: [Next Action] [Project] [Trash]. Selecting "Project" reveals an additional text input for the first action title. The existing context select is shared between Next Action and Project paths. No JS needed -- CSS `:has(:checked)` handles disclosure. **Option B -- Separate form per path:** Add a third form alongside the existing two. The "Project" form has: context select + first action title input + submit button. This keeps each form simple but makes each inbox item visually heavier. **Option C -- Dedicated clarify page:** Link from each inbox item to `/inbox/{id}/clarify` (a dedicated page) where radio buttons show all options with progressive disclosure. This is the approach mentioned in the product discovery doc (section 8, decision 7). ### My Recommendation: Option A (inline with radio buttons and CSS :has()) Reasons: - Matches UX Principle #4: "clarify must feel like sorting mail" -- no page navigation - Uses CSS `:has()` for progressive disclosure -- works without JS - The existing context select is already present; we just add a radio group and conditionally show a text input - Progressive enhancement: HTMX removes the item on submit, plain form redirects back to inbox - Accessible: `
`/`` wraps the radio buttons per Heydon's requirements The inbox item template would become: ```html
Clarify: {item.title}
``` The single form POSTs to `/inbox/{id}/clarify` and the handler branches on `clarify_type` to call either `clarify_as_next_action()` or a new `clarify_as_project()`. What do you all think? Particularly interested in feedback from: - **Heydon**: Is the fieldset/legend approach correct for a11y? Any concerns with `:has()`-based visibility for the project fields? - **Steve Schoger**: Does the visual weight work? Two radio buttons + conditionally revealed input. - **Scott**: Does the unified form endpoint make sense, or should we keep separate endpoints per clarify path? - **Luca**: Any concerns with branching the clarify handler based on a form field? - **Kent**: TDD approach -- should I start with the service layer test or the integration test?

content.text: Good -- Carson is asking for my accessibility feedback on the proposed interaction design. Let me think through this carefully before responding.

file.content: • {{ item.title }}

content.text: Now let me check CSS `:has()` browser support and think about the accessibility implications.

input.content:
Carson, good proposal. Here is my accessibility analysis of the three options and detailed feedback on Option A.

**Option A (inline radio + CSS `:has()`) -- I support this with some refinements.**

The `<fieldset>`/`<legend>` approach is correct. A radio group is semantically the right control for a mutually exclusive choice ("what kind of thing is this?"). Here are the specifics:

### 1. Fieldset/Legend structure -- correct, but refine the legend text

The legend should communicate the decision being made, not just name the item:
```html
<fieldset>
  <legend class="visually-hidden">Clarify: {{ item.title }}</legend>
```
This is good. "Clarify: Buy groceries" tells a screen reader user exactly what decision they are making.

### 2. CSS `:has()` for progressive disclosure -- accessible if done correctly

CSS `:has()` is well-supported (baseline since December 2023). Using it for show/hide is accessible **provided**:

- The hidden fields use `display: none` (not just `visibility: hidden` or `opacity: 0`), so they are removed from the accessibility tree when not relevant. This prevents screen readers from announcing "First action" input when the user has selected "Next Action" type.
- When the user switches from "Next Action" to "Project", the newly revealed input must not be `required` in HTML (since it would be required-but-hidden in the Next Action path). Instead, validate server-side. The server already knows from `clarify_type` whether `first_action_title` is needed.

Recommended CSS:
```css
.inbox-item__project-fields {
  display: none;
}

.inbox-item__clarify:has(input[value="project"]:checked) .inbox-item__project-fields {
  display: contents; /* or block/flex as needed */
}
```

### 3. The "first action" input needs a unique ID per inbox item

Since multiple inbox items are on the page simultaneously, each with its own form:
```html
<label for="first-action-{{ item.id }}">First action</label>
<input type="text" id="first-action-{{ item.id }}" name="first_action_title" ...>
```

### 4. Radio button labels must be visible

The radio buttons in your sketch use `<label><input type="radio"> Next Action</label>` -- this is correct. The wrapping `<label>` makes the text a click/tap target and provides the accessible name. Good.

### 5. The unified "Clarify" submit button -- accessible name concern

Your sketch shows a single `<button type="submit">Clarify</button>`. This needs a unique accessible name per item:
```html
<button type="submit" aria-label="Clarify: {{ item.title }}">Clarify</button>
```

However, I have a **concern about the button label change**. Currently the buttons read "Next Action" and "Trash" -- very direct, zero ambiguity about what will happen. Replacing these with a generic "Clarify" button means the action depends on which radio is selected. This is fine for sighted users who can see the radio state, but a screen reader user must remember which radio they selected before pressing "Clarify". 

**Alternative**: Keep the button text dynamic. When "Next Action" is selected, button says "Next Action". When "Project" is selected, button says "Create Project". This could be done with a small JS enhancement (progressive enhancement -- the fallback "Clarify" works fine without JS).

### 6. Trash should remain a separate form

The "Trash" action is destructive and conceptually different from "clarify into a destination list." Keeping it as a separate form (as it is now) is correct. Do not fold it into the radio group. Users should not accidentally trash an item by misclicking a radio button and then pressing submit.

### 7. What happens to the project title?

Your proposal says the inbox item title becomes the project title automatically. This is good -- no extra input needed. But make sure this is communicated to the user. The `<legend>` "Clarify: Buy groceries" already shows the item title, but when "Project" is selected, it might help to show a brief note: "Project title: Buy groceries" so the user knows what project will be created. This is a Minor/nice-to-have, not blocking.

### Summary of my position

**Option A is the right choice** with these refinements:
- `display: none` for hidden project fields (not opacity/visibility)
- Unique IDs per inbox item on all inputs
- Server-side validation for `first_action_title` (not HTML `required`)
- Keep Trash as a separate form
- Accessible name on the submit button including the item title
- Consider dynamic button text as a progressive enhancement

Options B and C are weaker: B adds visual weight without benefit (three separate forms per inbox item), and C breaks the "sorting mail" metaphor by requiring page navigation.

Ready to review the implementation when you have it.
input.summary: A11y review of Option A: support with 6 refinements

Your proposal says the inbox item title becomes the project title automatically. This is good -- no extra input needed. But make sure this is communicated to the user. The `<legend>` "Clarify: Buy groceries" already shows the item title, but when "Project" is selected, it might help to show a brief note: "Project title: Buy groceries" so the user knows what project will be created. This is a Minor/nice-to-have, not blocking.

### Summary of my position

**Option A is the right choice** with these refinements:
- `display: none` for hidden project fields (not opacity/visibility)
- Unique IDs per inbox item on all inputs
- Server-side validation for `first_action_title` (not HTML `required`)
- Keep Trash as a separate form
- Accessible name on the submit button including the item title
- Consider dynamic button text as a progressive enhancement

Options B and C are weaker: B adds visual weight without benefit (three separate forms per inbox item), and C breaks the "sorting mail" metaphor by requiring page navigation.

Ready to review the implementation when you have it."
  }
}

content.text:
Detailed accessibility feedback sent to Carson on the Option A interaction design. Key points:

- **Support Option A** (inline radio + CSS `:has()`) with refinements
- `<fieldset>`/`<legend>` is the correct semantic structure for the radio group
- Hidden project fields must use `display: none` to be removed from the accessibility tree
- No HTML `required` on conditionally-visible inputs -- validate server-side
- Unique IDs per inbox item on all form controls
- Accessible name on submit button including item title
- Keep Trash as a separate form (destructive action should not be in the radio group)
- Consider dynamic button text as progressive enhancement

Standing by for the implementation to review.
